text:
The challenged TFSAs did not "trade aggressively". That is pure spin put out by their tax advisors. The rich individuals almost certainly had large RRSPs or other taxable capital. Typically, they arranged for their RRSP to buy $5500 of a thinly traded stock from the TFSA at the published "ask" price, then immediately sell it back to the TFSA at the lower published "bid" price. A few cents stuck with the TFSA as a result. They repeated this transaction millions of times in a few seconds, as fast as the computer could whir, and thereby sucked all the money out of their RRSP into their TFSA.
The planning worked technically in those years -- the bid and ask prices are fair market value, and the Income Tax Act works off beneficial ownership, not registered ownership,so they did not even have to tell their broker what they were doing.
Anyone with a million-dollar TFSA that is being looked at by CRA who gives the "who, me?" innocent look is disingenuous.

text:
As for index ETFs outperforming index funds, that somewhat confusing paragraph is actually comparing index ETFs to the raw index (a theoretical entity). In a perfect world, index ETFs would match their underlying index, less fees, but in the real world they're not perfectly efficient due to periodic changes in the index and fluctuating numbers of units outstanding. The theoretical index makes changes instantaneously but the fund sponsor has to go to the market when attempting to fulfill their mandate of replicating it or issuing new units. When changes are announced in advance, there is an opportunity for arbitrageurs to trade on the change in demand of securities that are either being added or dropped from said index. The arbitrageurs profit at the expense of ETF unitholders, Smaller funds that track lesser known indices are especially vulnerable to this problem.

For more info see: https://www.bu.edu/rbfl/files/2013/09/Issue2McLaughlin.pdf

I’m grateful to the WHIDBEY NEWS-TIMES providing coverage of my study, and would like to add some further commentary on the article and the comments.

* I didn’t contact Mike Welding about the benefits of the Naval Air Station Whidbey Island (NASWI) because his information is, as he notes, “well known.”  It’s also obsolete. text:
Comments deleted for lack of civility are clearly noted in the thread. No stalking necessary.

The evidence for the link between warming and cyclones lies in basic physics. Cyclones are heat engines. They rely on a reservoir of heat to power convection and latent heat transfer. The more heat, the more likely the storm will intensify. AGW has warmed the oceans significantly.

Storm surge is likely the most dangerous impact of cyclones. AGW has increased sea level (and likely, the wind velocity of cyclones), which exacerbates the surge.

Extreme precipitation and flooding are yet another dangerous component of cyclones. The Clausius-Clapeyron relation clearly points to an increase in precipitable water potential as a result of warming. Warmer often results in wetter.

The term "cyclone" (more accurately, "tropical cyclone") is the generic designation for all cyclones, typhoons, and hurricanes. Different location, different name, but "cyclone" covers them all.

It's not "I don't want to pay for your x, y, or z" It's "I don't need someone to pay for MY x, y, or z" therefore my cost should go down because I am assuming the risk.  No one is saying they want an option to make maternity and pediatric coverage unavailable.  They are saying they want to allow people to buy insurance that does not cover things they do not need to cover.  

I'd much rather let a 20-something single man buy insurance for himself at a price he can afford to cover what actually could happen to him, such as cancer, appendicitis, a broken leg, etc. than force him to buy coverage for pregnancy and childbirth at a price he can't afford.

If you want "health care cost sharing", you are free to find an organization out there that does it and join them. They exist.  Cost sharing is not the same as insurance.  Insurance is purchased to cover risks you cannot financially cover yourself.  It is priced based on the probability you will incur that covered cost.

This is fallacious thinking. In particular, it's an example of "begging the question." 

The PCA did not reach the conclusion that the Kingdom of Hawaii still exists. What happened is that some guy (who happens to be a felon) made a conditional claim. He said, essentially: if P then Q. The court (because it's a whore for money and attention) allowed the *conditional* existence of P, but did not decide if Q was or was not. They didn't need solid evidence; all they needed was a plausible argument based on a lot of assumptions, and a boatload of filing fees. 

By suggesting that the PCA's initial *conditional* acceptance of the lawsuits' filing determines anything at all (or represents a conclusion), you're begging the question.

More to the point, the PCA has zero power to enforce or interpret any law whatsoever. It facilitates arbitration. At this point, I do wonder if that's actually a "court" at all. It's not an authority; it's a bunch of people in a room grinding axes.

text: Where does all the money go that is given by taxpayers to indigenous communities? The only reports provided by the mainstream media focus on alleged under funding in certain areas. But if examined on the basis of total funding per capita to indigenous communities, aboriginal communities receive far, far more than is provided to non-aboriginals. For example, Nunavut (created to be a model of aboriginal self-government) receives per capita annual funding from the federal taxpayers of $42,055 (yes, that's right, a family of four in Nunavut receives annual support of $168,000 from federal taxpayers). That compares to the per-capita funding from the federal government for all Canadians of $1,986. Federal funding for Nunavut per capita is over 20 times greater than the average for all Canadians. To begin with, what the public thinks of the newsworthiness of a story is irrelevant. The newsworthiness of a particular event is determined by editors and to a minor extent reporters. In more than 20 years of being a freelance journalist, I was never told by an editor to take a poll of a sliver of the public to determine if I should go ahead and cover a particular story or not. 
Secondly, the issue isn't whether the Billy Kenoi drunken toast "scandal" isn't newsworthy per se but whether it was a significant enough story in and of itself. 
Clearly from my perspective it was newsworthy. However, it wasn't much of a news story.
Finally, Brett's point on individual decisions in the news media is an excellent one and sadly, poorly understood by the public. Most people see the news media as plural and not singular. They don't understand that different newspapers, TV stations, websites make different decisions everyday.
For Example: Take the NY Times Story on Trump's taxes.

text:
As I've had to point out to others, "vehicle damage" is only a slice of the total cost of providing roads.  Building the roads and bridges in the first place is the biggest expense, while weathering (especially the freeze/thaw cycles in Colorado), shifting soils, snow plowing, sewer/utility line work, etc all contribute to the need for maintenance costs as well. Vehicle damage is PART of the cost, but only part.  

Obviously the usage fees/taxes for bikes using the shared roads should be less than the usage taxes/fees for motor vehicles.  Nobody is suggesting they should be as much.  But that doesn't mean they should be ZERO.  Unless you plan to make them zero for motor vehicles too and pay for the whole thing out of general funds that everyone pays regardless.  

Beyond that, we have the issue of dedicated bike lanes and paths that only bikes are allowed to use, which is an even more obvious case for bike-specific taxes.

text:
MA: Donkel's position is understandable ... the minor oilies don't have the capital to finance feeder lines to TAPS, and the major oilies aren't offering any help. It's a shut-out and the State is too busy fawning over the majors, leaving the minors hung out to twist in the wind. Were the majors making any moves to explore where the minors are doing so, then I would have little sympathy for Donkel and friends ... but their not. All that oil is in individual fields too small to interest the majors, and if it weren't for the minors, it would probably stay there forever, not bringing any revenue to Alaska.
Collectively, the small fields discovered so far, combined with other as of yet undiscovered fields, could easily far exceed Prudhoe bay. Some estimates of tar sands already exceed Prudhoe Bay, by far.
Alaska could finance those feeder lines, simply by guaranteeing a percentage of the loans, much like VA home loans. The loans could be paid off by Alaska taking a percentage of the oil.

text: The very concept of "sweater" is a cultural appropriation, in this context--but also an example of cultural exchange. Knitting was introduced to the indigenous women by, among others, missionary nuns. An immigrant woman from the Shetlands is credited with the idea of adding designs to the garments the women were producing. And soon the women themselves were "appropriating" (and adapting) the designs they saw on sweaters worn by the local--British--fishermen. Apparently, the actual knitting technique is itself an adaptation (not mere "adoption") of the Shetland techniques; that's what makes the sweaters unique. When in the 1940s a Manitoba company began to mass-produce Cowichan sweater "knock-offs," ironically a number of the indigenous knitters quickly incorporated their own versions of the "commercialized" designs into their work. Are the sweaters "indigenous cultural items"? Or would today's SJW's call them "symbols of oppression," forced upon the indigenous people by the whites?

text:
Drought? Warmer temps also mean that moisture is evaporated out of the soil faster than at cooler temps. This is probably one reason why fire season has gotten longer; hotter temps have created better conditions for burns for more of the year.  

And greater evaporation from the oceans doesn't necessarily mean the rain will fall where it's needed to prevent fires or irrigate crops.  Warmer ocean temps, for instance, undoubtedly supercharged Harvey and provided more moisture for precipitation, but when that rain came what did it do? Where did it go? 

It destroyed or ruined tens of thousands of houses and businesses, killed a score of people, created preconditions for epidemics,  and then flowed back into the ocean. Some of it might have been absorbed into the ground--hence mitigating droughts--but over an extensively paved area like Houston the vast majority of it probably ran off into rivers and bayous flowing into the Gulf.

text: Thomas, I think we have a misunderstanding here. For several hundred thousand years the earth has been in thermal balance, meaning that solar energy hitting the earth is roughly equal to outgoing energy due to the earth's  temperature. Greenhouse gases have created an imbalance of approx 2 watts per square meter on average over the earth's surface. That imbalance will continue until the earth warms up enough to achieve balance once again. A lot of people don't understand this dynamic. If you would like to learn more, a good place to start is here: http://history.aip.org/climate/summary.htm.

Broken down:

Support was 82% among Democrats, 70% among Republicans, and 72% among independents.

By age, support was 67% among 18-29 year olds, 68% among 30-45 year olds, 82% among 46-65 year olds, and 76% for those older than 65.

By gender, support was 81% among women and 71% among men.

In Gallup polls since they started asking in 1944 until this election, only about 20% of the public has supported the current system of awarding all of a state's electoral votes to the presidential candidate who receives the most votes in each separate state (not mentioned in the U.S. Constitution, but later enacted by 48 states) (with about 70% opposed and about 10% undecided).

text:
The issue of paying RRSP/TFSA fees outside these accounts is a bit of a distraction. Sure it allows your investments inside these accounts to grow more but the $100 a year will not get you far unless one does not have  much in these accounts in the first place, in which case you should talk to your plan provider and get a fee reduction. If they will not give you one switch providers.

A few years back I read stories that said the average RRSP account was about $95,000 and the average TFSA around $13,000. A fee of $100 on these accounts comes to about 0.1% and 0.75% respectively. Obviously there is more benefit to paying the fee outside a TFSA but again, get your plan provider to reduce the fee first.

Tax on this fee, if it was taxed as a benefit, would amount to about $30 for the average person and maybe $45 for a high income earner per account.  Taxing fees paid outside these programs is not likely to be a big revenue generator for the CRA and not worth the hassle.

text:
Isn't passive investment income earned in a corporation subject to Part IV tax (dividends) and other high rate taxes (i.e. there is no small business deduction for investment income)?  In effect, in Alberta for example, the initial corporate tax rate is 50.67% on passive investment income (interest, dividends, rent, royalties) as compared to a 48% highest marginal personal tax rate on investment income (for income earners over $300,000 of income).  Therefore, this article, and other people (govt and tax advisors) are not really giving the full picture here.  
There are two issues.  Shareholders get taxed at small business rate 12% in Alberta.  If they take out the money as dividends, they are taxed at 48% altogether.  There is a 36% deferral, money that can be left in the company to compound.  In the end, CRA will get more money, it is just deferred.  The other issue is some business owners may be treating passive investment income as business income (incorrect tax filing).  Thoughts?

Correct. A decision by the county commissioners cannot be "appealed" in court, because the commissioners are not a court and cannot render legal decisions. A lawsuit can be filed in Circuit Court to contest a decision by county commissioners, and if unsuccessful, could be appealed to the Oregon Court of Appeals and eventually to the State Supreme Court. No District Court involved at any step along the way.

Even in the unlikely event that a state electoral matter was decided in a federal court, it still would not be "appealed in District Court," because an appeal would take place in the US Court of Appeals (9th circuit), which is not a District Court but has appellate jurisdiction over it.

text:
Mr Todd, There is not a justified aquifer contamination argument. This really does not have any scientific support.

Have you read Dr. Thomas's papers about the age of water in aquifers (and how water is filtered throughout the system, and how many toxic elements are naturally eroding into the system, then filtered or diluted in a HUGE aquifer system)? The tradewind inversion is usually capped at 9,000ft on Mauna Kea. 80-200inches of rain occurs per year below that line throughout different broad areas on Hawaii Island. This is where the aquifer recharge occurs, not the summit of Mauna Kea. 10-20 in. of rain is what typically occurs above 9000ft. In Dr. Thomas's studies, the age of the water in the aquifer is ~9,500 years old. It will take thousands of years (and major filtering and chemical interactions) as the water moves down to ~3,000ft on Big Island toward the aquifer if it ever makes it down from the summit. It will also be vastly diluted by water from below the cloudline.

text:
Not sure what the logic is behind the assertion you should only have US stocks in an RRSP?

The only relevant factor of when it comes to an RRSP or any registered account is the tax implication.  Generally speaking you want to avoid holding stocks or investments in an RRSP if upon withdrawal the money will be subject to a higher rate of tax.

The only difference (for your typical investor) between US stocks and Canadian stocks in this regard is the taxation of eligible Canadian dividends.  So while it may make more sense to keep Canadian dividend stocks in cash account or TFSA that doesn't equate to saying your RRSP should only hold US stocks.  It really depends on your current and expected future tax rate, as well as your overall portfolio composition.

text:
Lack of care for our local pod of killer whales and the sea and land we share share with them results in no wild salmon dinner for our kids, or theirs. Watching these beautiful, intelligent and social whales every summer from the entry to Active Pass, we have seen the decline in orca numbers and health that researchers predicted. It's clear that we are losing them. The Center for Whale Research says six new southern resident killer whales were born in 2015 and 2016, but J52 is the third of that group to die, likely of starvation,and none of the whales born this year has survived.

Every summer we observe fast, deafening, GPS-guided whalewatching boats that, both legally, and endorsed by our tourism industry, surround every transiting whale family to give customers the best view. Out of public view, we have seen boaters enclose mothers with infants in a tightening ring of exhaust-beltching, roaring boats that followed them everywhere. You try fishing in those conditions. Loved to death.

What alarmed Pope John Paul about the pastoral letters on peace and on economic justice was NOT the content of the letters, but the process the bishops devised for writing them. The content of both letters was mainstream, traditional Catholic teaching. In some respects, John Paul's own teaching on those issues was to the left of the American bishops. John Paul objected to the method: the bishops established a process for listening to a variety of voices -- experts in relevant fields as well as representatives of various segments of the church -- as they drafted the texts of the letters.  Imagine!  Bishops listening, bishops learning, bishops acknowledging that they didn't already have all the answers! (What if they wrote about marriage? What if they -- gasp! -- listened to married persons?)

text:
Having Irish Catholic ancestors, I wanted to share some observations regarding the words FATHER and MOTHER.    My parents were born in America in the early 1900s.  Apparently, the word FATHER was traditionally reserved for priests, whereas the word MOTHER was proper for female parents.

Irish Catholics usually seemed to prefer “dad”, or less often, “da”.  I knew kids who used the word “pop”, but none seemed to be of Irish descent.  Some of my Protestant friends used the word “father” to address their male parent, which seemed weird to me as a child.

My mother and aunts usually used the word “mother” to address their female parents.  Although my mother would have liked me to do the same, times had changed and I insisted on caller her “mom”.  She endured this generational shift, but did make it clear that calling her “ma” or “mummy” would be disrespectful.

Incidentally, during my early childhood, our pastor was called “Monsignor”, which seems to be equivalent to “My Lord”.

"Among immigrants in low income during their first full year in Canada, from one-third to two-fifths of their spells were chronic, in the sense that they lasted at least all of the first five years in Canada. It wouldn't be good for the country and it they would find a country just as wretched as what they left.  

We need to move away from the chain migration system that has evolved, and pay more attention to protecting opportunities for Americans and legal immigrants.
